SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper runs experiments against multiple state-of-the-art baselines on two standard datasets (Twitter15, Twitter16) and reports early detection accuracy (≈75% / 73% within 24 hours), which provides reasonable empirical support for the main claims. However, key analyses are missing: no ablation or sensitivity studies are reported and hyperparameters are not provided, impairing understanding of what drives performance. Some limitations are acknowledged (dataset construction by selecting only popular source tweets, ignoring unpopular tweets, and data collection issues via the Twitter API), but these choices leave an important coverage gap and reproducibility is further weakened by lack of code or detailed settings. | B(3): The paper runs experiments against several strong baselines (SVM-TS, DTR, DTC, GRU, etc.) on two public datasets (Twitter15, Twitter16) and reports timely accuracy gains (~75% and ~73% within 24 hours), which provides reasonable empirical support. However, no ablation or sensitivity analyses are reported and hyperparameters/code are not provided, limiting understanding of which components (PTK vs cPTK, temporal/structural/linguistic signals) drive performance and hindering reproducibility. Related work coverage is said to be thorough and some limitations (dataset construction choices, ignoring unpopular tweets, API collection issues) are discussed, but the lack of methodological detail and ablations means important analyses are missing.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper runs experiments against multiple state-of-the-art baselines on two standard datasets (Twitter15, Twitter16) and reports early detection accuracy (≈75% / 73% within 24 hours), which provides reasonable empirical support for the main claims. However, key analyses are missing: no ablation or sensitivity studies are reported and hyperparameters are not provided, impairing understanding of what drives performance. Some limitations are acknowledged (dataset construction by selecting only popular source tweets, ignoring unpopular tweets, and data collection issues via the Twitter API), but these choices leave an important coverage gap and reproducibility is further weakened by lack of code or detailed settings.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper runs experiments against several strong baselines (SVM-TS, DTR, DTC, GRU, etc.) on two public datasets (Twitter15, Twitter16) and reports timely accuracy gains (~75% and ~73% within 24 hours), which provides reasonable empirical support. However, no ablation or sensitivity analyses are reported and hyperparameters/code are not provided, limiting understanding of which components (PTK vs cPTK, temporal/structural/linguistic signals) drive performance and hindering reproducibility. Related work coverage is said to be thorough and some limitations (dataset construction choices, ignoring unpopular tweets, API collection issues) are discussed, but the lack of methodological detail and ablations means important analyses are missing.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an important problem—early rumor detection on social media—and proposes a concrete, interpretable modeling approach (PTK and cPTK) that combines temporal, structural and linguistic signals. Empirical gains (≈75% on Twitter15 and 73% on Twitter16 within 24 hours) over several strong baselines suggest the approach could change how researchers model propagation for early detection and be adopted by practitioners. The method’s kernel-based framing and focus on propagation trees open useful new directions for exploitation of diffusion structure. However, reliance on reshaped datasets that exclude unpopular tweets and data-collection limitations may constrain immediate generalizability, tempering transformative impact to strong but not field-changing. | B(4): The paper addresses an important problem—early rumor detection on microblogs—by introducing a Propagation Tree Kernel (PTK) and context-sensitive cPTK that explicitly model propagation tree structure and combine temporal, structural and linguistic signals. Empirically it outperforms several state-of-the-art baselines and achieves about 75% (Twitter15) and 73% (Twitter16) accuracy within 24 hours, suggesting practical gains for faster detection. This kernel-based, structure-aware approach is likely to change how researchers model diffusion for rumor detection and to inspire follow-up work on propagation-structure methods. However, reliance on popular tweets only and data-collection limitations temper its immediate, transformative reach across all settings.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an important problem—early rumor detection on social media—and proposes a concrete, interpretable modeling approach (PTK and cPTK) that combines temporal, structural and linguistic signals. Empirical gains (≈75% on Twitter15 and 73% on Twitter16 within 24 hours) over several strong baselines suggest the approach could change how researchers model propagation for early detection and be adopted by practitioners. The method’s kernel-based framing and focus on propagation trees open useful new directions for exploitation of diffusion structure. However, reliance on reshaped datasets that exclude unpopular tweets and data-collection limitations may constrain immediate generalizability, tempering transformative impact to strong but not field-changing.

Empirically it outperforms several state-of-the-art baselines and achieves about 75% (Twitter15) and 73% (Twitter16) accuracy within 24 hours, suggesting practical gains for faster detection. This kernel-based, structure-aware approach is likely to change how researchers model diffusion for rumor detection and to inspire follow-up work on propagation-structure methods. However, reliance on popular tweets only and data-collection limitations temper its immediate, transformative reach across all settings.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper’s main claims (PTK/cPTK models and improved early rumor detection, ~75% on Twitter15 and ~73% on Twitter16) are supported by experiments against reasonable baselines. However, important methodological/reporting gaps weaken technical confidence: hyperparameters are not reported, code/datasets are not publicly provided, unpopular tweets were explicitly excluded (introducing selection bias), and only accuracy is reported with no mention of statistical testing. Given these concerns the results are suggestive but not fully convincing, so the technical soundness is moderate rather than flawless. | B(3): The paper states clear main claims (PTK/cPTK kernels, kernel-SVM classification) and reports empirical support against multiple baselines on Twitter15/16 (≈75% and 73% within 24h). However, important methodological concerns remain: the authors constructed datasets by selecting only popular source tweets and explicitly ignored unpopular tweets, introducing selection bias; data completeness is uncertain because Twitter API limitations required web crawling; hyperparameters are not reported and code is not mentioned, hindering reproducibility; and no mention of statistical significance testing or additional evaluation metrics. These issues raise moderate concerns about the experimental methodology and generalizability but do not definitively invalidate the core technical contribution.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s main claims (PTK/cPTK models and improved early rumor detection, ~75% on Twitter15 and ~73% on Twitter16) are supported by experiments against reasonable baselines. However, important methodological/reporting gaps weaken technical confidence: hyperparameters are not reported, code/datasets are not publicly provided, unpopular tweets were explicitly excluded (introducing selection bias), and only accuracy is reported with no mention of statistical testing. Given these concerns the results are suggestive but not fully convincing, so the technical soundness is moderate rather than flawless.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper states clear main claims (PTK/cPTK kernels, kernel-SVM classification) and reports empirical support against multiple baselines on Twitter15/16 (≈75% and 73% within 24h). However, important methodological concerns remain: the authors constructed datasets by selecting only popular source tweets and explicitly ignored unpopular tweets, introducing selection bias; data completeness is uncertain because Twitter API limitations required web crawling; hyperparameters are not reported and code is not mentioned, hindering reproducibility; and no mention of statistical significance testing or additional evaluation metrics. These issues raise moderate concerns about the experimental methodology and generalizability but do not definitively invalidate the core technical contribution.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper appears well organized with clear early statement of contributions (PTK and cPTK) and a logical method→experiments→conclusion flow; writing quality is noted as good and related work coverage is thorough. Presentation has minor issues: hyperparameters are not reported and code availability is not mentioned, and the summary does not indicate whether figures/tables are provided or especially informative. These omissions suggest small presentation gaps but do not detract from generally clear communication. | B(4): The paper appears well organized with a clear flow from problem to method to experiments and explicitly states its main contributions early (the PTK and cPTK propagation-kernel approaches for source-tweet rumor classification). Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage is thorough, making the presentation generally easy to follow; the methodology and datasets (Twitter15/16) and reported results (≈75%/73% within 24h) are described. Minor presentation issues remain: hyperparameters are not reported and code availability is not mentioned, and no information was provided about the effectiveness of figures/tables, which slightly reduces reproducibility and clarity of presentation.

Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage is thorough, making the presentation generally easy to follow; the methodology and datasets (Twitter15/16) and reported results (≈75%/73% within 24h) are described. APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The topic—rumor detection on social media using propagation structure—is squarely within NLP/ML venue scope and targets the right audience (social NLP, misinformation). The paper formulates a clear problem and proposes a modeling approach (PTK/cPTK kernels embedded in SVM) evaluated on standard Twitter15/Twitter16 datasets against relevant baselines, which matches community expectations. Minor concerns about evaluation breadth and reproducibility (accuracy-only reporting, no code/hyperparameter details, and explicitly ignoring unpopular tweets plus data-collection caveats) limit it from being a perfect fit but it remains a good fit for a top venue. | B(4): This paper addresses rumor detection on microblogs using propagation-tree kernels (PTK, cPTK) and kernel SVMs, which is squarely within the NLP/ML community interested in misinformation and diffusion modeling. It evaluates on standard Twitter15/Twitter16 datasets and compares to relevant baselines, making it appropriate for a top venue audience. Minor concerns for a top-tier ML conference are the largely application-driven focus and some reporting gaps (no code or hyperparameter disclosure, and ignoring unpopular tweets), but overall the problem, datasets, and evaluation align well with venue expectations.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper proposes new kernel functions (Propagation Tree Kernel and its context-sensitive variant) to directly model and compare propagation trees, and unifies temporal, structural and linguistic signals in a kernel-based SVM framework—a concrete methodological novelty beyond standard feature- or RNN-based approaches. The authors also construct propagation-tree datasets from Twitter15/16 and explicitly target early, source-tweet-level classification, distinguishing the work from the listed baselines. Rumor detection is a well-studied problem, but the specific tree-kernel formulation and context-sensitive extension represent a non-trivial, notable contribution that would likely inform experts in the field. | B(4): The paper introduces a specific Propagation Tree Kernel (PTK) and a context-sensitive variant (cPTK) to model diffusion trees—this kernel-based modeling of propagation structure for rumor detection is a clearly new methodological contribution compared to the listed baselines (including feature-based classifiers and RNNs). Rumor detection itself is a well-studied problem, but unifying temporal, structural and linguistic signals in a propagation-tree kernel and embedding it into an SVM is a non-trivial, clearly differentiated extension. The work is notable and would likely inform experts, though it is not a radical paradigm shift in the area.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes new kernel functions (Propagation Tree Kernel and its context-sensitive variant) to directly model and compare propagation trees, and unifies temporal, structural and linguistic signals in a kernel-based SVM framework—a concrete methodological novelty beyond standard feature- or RNN-based approaches. The authors also construct propagation-tree datasets from Twitter15/16 and explicitly target early, source-tweet-level classification, distinguishing the work from the listed baselines. Rumor detection is a well-studied problem, but the specific tree-kernel formulation and context-sensitive extension represent a non-trivial, notable contribution that would likely inform experts in the field.

Rumor detection itself is a well-studied problem, but unifying temporal, structural and linguistic signals in a propagation-tree kernel and embedding it into an SVM is a non-trivial, clearly differentiated extension. MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against multiple relevant and relatively recent baselines (SVM-TS 2015, DTR 2015, DTC 2011, SVM-RBF 2012, RFC 2017, GRU 2016) and evaluates on standard Twitter15/Twitter16 data. However, the authors constructed a filtered dataset by extracting only popular source tweets and explicitly ignored unpopular tweets, and they do not report code or hyperparameters, raising concerns about fairness and reproducibility of comparisons. The related work coverage is described as thorough, but the paper does not explicitly note when prior methods perform comparably or better. Overall this is a good comparison set with minor omissions and fairness/reproducibility issues. | B(4): The paper evaluates against a solid set of prior methods (SVM-TS 2015, DTR 2015, DTC 2011, SVM-RBF 2012, RFC 2017, GRU 2016) on the same public benchmarks (Twitter15, Twitter16) using accuracy, so comparisons appear broadly appropriate and relevant. However, the summary notes missing details that affect fairness/reproducibility (no code availability and hyperparameters not reported) and does not indicate explicit acknowledgement when baselines perform comparably, so there are minor omissions despite thorough related-work coverage.

main_claims: Proposes a Propagation Tree Kernel (PTK) and a context-sensitive variant (cPTK) to model propagation tree structures of microblog posts for rumor detection.
Combines temporal, structural and linguistic signals in a unified kernel-based framework to distinguish false rumor, true rumor, unverified rumor and non-rumor at the source-tweet level.

Demonstrates that the kernel-based approach detects rumors more quickly and more accurately than several state-of-the-art baselines, achieving about 75% accuracy on Twitter15 and 73% on Twitter16 within 24 hours.
_summary.methodology: Represent microblog diffusion as propagation trees and compute similarities between trees using the proposed PTK and cPTK; embed these kernel functions into a kernel-based SVM (one-vs-all) to perform multi-class classification on source tweets.
limitations: No public dataset existed for classifying propagation trees; authors constructed datasets by extracting popular source tweets from Twitter15 and Twitter16.
Unpopular tweets were ignored (explicitly stated: "Though unpopular tweets could be false, we ignore them").
Twitter API cannot retrieve retweets or replies, requiring use of external tools (Twrench) and web crawling for data collection.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - rumor detection / social media misinformation
